narrative: As a white male applicant with a solid middle-income family background, your law school application was evaluated using a model trained on past students' data to predict bar exam success on the first try. The model assessed your Year 1 school decile of 2—well below the typical 5.7 average—your Year 3 decile of 5, roughly in line with the average of 5.5, your LSAT score of 22 compared to the usual 37, and your undergraduate GPA of 2.4 against the average 3.2, along with your plan to attend full-time like most students. It predicted a 45% chance that you would fail the bar exam on your first attempt, which crossed the threshold for rejection to minimize the risk of admitting students unlikely to pass. The three biggest factors driving this decision were your Year 3 performance ranking, your LSAT score, and your family income level. Most notably, your Year 3 decile worked strongly in your favor, suggesting that improvement pushed the model toward seeing you as more likely to pass, unlike many applicants who stay lower-ranked. On the flip side, your LSAT score had the strongest pull toward failure, as it was significantly lower than what the model sees in successful students, outweighing other positives. Your middle-income bracket offered a modest boost toward passing, aligning with patterns where such backgrounds correlate slightly with better outcomes. Compared to typical admitted students, your earlier weaker Year 1 standing and standout low LSAT set you apart from the higher-achieving profiles the model favors for bar success. While full-time status is common and neutral here, it didn't offset the key drags. This rejection stings, especially given your upward trend in later years, but it's not the end—many students in similar spots rebound by retaking the LSAT to hit 30-plus or bolstering their GPA through post-bac programs. Consider targeted prep courses proven to lift scores for students like you, and reapply after showing clear progress. You've already climbed from a low Year 1 decile, so building on that resilience could flip the script next time.
